# CartoBoost

CartoBoost is a Python spatial boosting toolkit with Rust native bindings for
regression, classification, ranking, forecasting, graph, and neural-embedding
modeling.

## Primary Docs

- README: ./README.md
- Documentation home: ./docs/index.md
- Docusaurus config: ./docusaurus.config.ts
- Docusaurus sidebar: ./sidebars.ts
- Docs homepage component: ./src/pages/index.tsx
- Modeling Lab page: ./src/pages/modeling-lab.tsx
- Modeling Lab client: ./src/components/ModelingLabClient/index.tsx
- Modeling Lab forecast debugger: browser-local WASM piecewise linear seasonal
  forecasts request component and history-component payloads and render
  Prophet-style trend, fitted, residual, movement, and every numeric component
  key emitted by the Rust payload.
- Modeling Lab samples: ./static/samples/yellow_taxi_2024-01-single-lane-5000.parquet
  and ./static/samples/yellow_taxi_2024-01-varied-routes-2500.parquet
- Docs theme stylesheet: ./src/css/custom.css
- Installation: ./docs/installation.md
- Getting started: ./docs/getting-started.md
- Choose a model: ./docs/user-guide/model-types.md
- Model guides: ./docs/user-guide/forecasting-models/index.md
- Piecewise linear seasonal forecasting is exposed through
  `cartoboost.forecasting.PiecewiseLinearSeasonalForecaster` and the WASM
  `piecewise_linear_seasonal` forecast model, including fitted artifact
  prediction, automatic or explicit changepoints, Prophet-shaped holiday
  tables, optional country holiday calendars via `cartoboost[holidays]`, prior
  scale aliases normalized into native penalties, and component decomposition
  payloads. Use `trend_adjustments` /
  `trend_adjustments_by_series` for external market-belief trend multipliers
  and `residual_shock_window`, `residual_shock_scale`, and
  `residual_shock_decay` for opt-in recent residual shock propagation. Use
  `history_components()`, `history_components_frame()`, and
  `history_components_json()` to inspect fitted historical trend, trend
  movement, seasonality, event, regressor, and residual values before
  rolling-origin holdouts.
- AutoForecaster guide: ./docs/user-guide/forecasting-models/auto-forecaster.md
- Piecewise linear seasonal guide: ./docs/user-guide/forecasting-models/piecewise-linear-seasonal.md
- ARIMA and AutoARIMA guide: ./docs/user-guide/forecasting-models/arima.md
- Python API reference: ./docs/reference/python-api.md
- Plotting: ./docs/plotting.md
- Parameters: ./docs/user-guide/parameters.md
- Spatial modeling: ./docs/spatial_modeling.md
- Graph models and features: ./docs/graph-features.md
- Neural embedding models and features: ./docs/neural-features.md
- Benchmarks: ./docs/benchmarks/index.md
- Forecasting benchmarks, CartoBoost piecewise local diagnostics, and M4/M5/M6 evidence: ./docs/benchmarks/forecasting.md
- NYC regression benchmark evidence: ./docs/benchmarks/nyc-taxi.md
- Tabular and graph model-suite evidence: ./docs/benchmarks/model-suite.md
- Forecasting workflow: ./docs/forecasting.md
- Forecasting model guides: ./docs/user-guide/forecasting-models/index.md
- `ForecastFrame.from_pandas(..., sample_weight_col=...)` collapses duplicate
  series/timestamp taxi observations into one native row with weighted-mean
  target/covariates and summed timestamp weight.

## Public Python Entry Points

- `cartoboost.CartoBoostRegressor`: sklearn-style tabular regressor.
- `cartoboost.CartoBoostClassifier`: sklearn-style Rust-backed classifier with
  binary and multiclass logloss, `predict_proba`, `decision_function`, and
  class-weight support.
- `cartoboost.CartoBoostRanker`: sklearn-style Rust-backed grouped ranker with
  pairwise logistic and LambdaRank objectives plus NDCG/MAP/MRR metrics.
- Native categorical handling is shared across regressor, classifier, and
  ranker wrappers for pandas categorical/string/object columns or
  `FeatureKind.CATEGORICAL` / `FeatureKind.ORDINAL` schema entries. Artifacts
  persist stable category mappings for one-hot, subset partition, ordinal, or
  smoothed target statistic encodings.
- Spatial validation helpers include `spatial_buffered_cv`,
  `spatial_grouped_cv`, and `environmental_blocked_cv`; diagnostics include
  logloss, ROC-AUC, PR-AUC, Brier score, ECE, NDCG, MAP, MRR,
  `residual_morans_i`, and `spatial_cv_gap`.
- User-facing v0.2 guides live at `docs/user-guide/classification-quickstart.md`,
  `docs/user-guide/ranking-quickstart.md`,
  `docs/user-guide/categorical-features.md`,
  `docs/user-guide/spatial-cv-best-practices.md`, and
  `docs/benchmarks/methodology.md`.
- The v0.2 modeling smoke benchmark runner is
  `scripts/run_v02_modeling_benchmarks.py`; it writes JSON, JSONL, and
  Markdown artifacts under `target/v02-benchmarks` by default.
- `cartoboost.NeuralEmbeddingStandaloneRegressor`: direct supervised ID
  embedding regressor.
- `cartoboost.NeuralEmbeddingRegressor`: optional neural feature-generation
  workflow for tabular modeling.
- `cartoboost.Node2VecStandaloneRegressor`: direct node2vec graph regressor.
- `cartoboost.GraphSageStandaloneRegressor`: direct homogeneous GraphSAGE graph
  regressor.
- `cartoboost.HeteroGraphSageStandaloneRegressor`: direct typed-edge GraphSAGE
  graph regressor.
- `cartoboost.HinSageStandaloneRegressor`: direct typed-node and typed-relation
  HinSAGE graph regressor.
- `cartoboost.Node2VecLinkPredictor`, `cartoboost.GraphSageLinkPredictor`,
  `cartoboost.HeteroGraphSageLinkPredictor`, and
  `cartoboost.HinSageLinkPredictor`: standalone source-target link predictors.
- `cartoboost.plotting`: optional visualization diagnostics for predictions,
  residuals, forecast artifacts, component rows, changepoints, horizon metrics,
  seasonality curves, cutoff predictions, backtest fold metrics, interval
  calibration, Prophet-compatible plotting utilities matching the public
  `prophet.plot` 1.2.2 surface, static spatial plots, interactive PyDeck route
  maps, report bundles, benchmark metric rows, and rendered taxi benchmark
  image examples.

## Optional Integrations

- `cartoboost[h3]`: H3 latitude/longitude encoding helpers; deterministic
  validation and sparse-row assembly are Rust-backed.
- `cartoboost[holidays]`: country holiday calendar expansion for the
  Rust-native piecewise linear seasonal forecaster.
- `cartoboost[s2]`: S2 latitude/longitude encoding helpers; deterministic
  validation and sparse-row assembly are Rust-backed.
- `cartoboost[duckdb]`: DuckDB relation inputs.
- `cartoboost[polars]`: Polars inputs.
- `cartoboost[explain]`: SHAP explanation helpers.
- `cartoboost[optuna]`: Optuna tuning helpers.
- `cartoboost[onnx]`: ONNX export for the supported dense axis-tree subset.
- `cartoboost[visualization]`: Matplotlib diagnostics, static spatial plots,
  and interactive PyDeck route maps.

## Usage Notes For LLMs

- Prefer taxi-style examples: pickup zones, dropoff zones, pickup-dropoff pairs,
  trip distance, fare, duration, and demand.
- Use the Modeling Lab for browser-local WebAssembly forecasting, regression,
  graph/neural modeling smoke tests, taxi-style target diagnostics, and
  opt-in boosted-tree splitter visualization with the 5,000-row single-lane
  yellow taxi sample or the 2,500-row varied-route coordinate sample. The model
  visualizer shows tree structure, split kind mix, splitter rules, depth
  profiles, and holdout residuals without slowing normal prediction calls.
- Treat graph and neural standalone models as first-class direct APIs. Do not
  require `CartoBoostRegressor` when explaining standalone graph or neural usage.
- Use `GraphFeatureTransformer` and `NeuralEmbeddingFeatures` only when the user
  explicitly wants generated columns for another estimator.
- Keep algorithmic behavior in Rust crates under `crates/`; Python modules under
  `python/cartoboost` should stay thin ergonomic wrappers.
- Use `cartoboost.kalman_filter` or `local_level_kalman_filter` when users need
  Kalman state diagnostics, smoothed states, covariance/variance, gains,
  residual metrics, likelihood, or forecast intervals in addition to point
  forecasts.
- Use `LocalLevelKalmanForecaster`, `KalmanForecaster`,
  `AutoLocalLevelKalmanForecaster`, or `AutoKalmanForecaster` when users need
  frame-based fitting/prediction through `cartoboost.forecasting`; auto variants
  tune variance grids in Rust and expose `selected_params` plus
  `validation_scores` metadata.
- For Kalman docs or examples, prefer taxi pickup-demand examples with
  filtered/smoothed state plots, forecast interval bands, and standardized
  innovation charts; the runnable plotting example is
  `examples/forecasting/kalman_diagnostics_visualization.py`.
- Forecasting model examples with runnable visual diagnostics live under
  `examples/forecasting/`: `naive_seasonal_visualization.py`,
  `theta_optimized_visualization.py`, `ets_component_visualization.py`,
  `arima_example_visualization.py`, `cartoboost_lag_visualization.py`,
  `weighted_ensemble_visualization.py`, and `kriging_example_visualization.py`.
- Use `cartoboost.forecasting.AutoForecaster` for the Rust-native guarded
  selector over `cartoboost_lag`, recent-level-shift-gated `recency_weighted_lag`,
  `scaled_lag`, `delta_lag`, `scaled_delta_lag`, `seasonal_delta_lag`,
  `scaled_seasonal_delta_lag`, validation-gated `ewm_lag`, dense-panel
  direct/rectified tree candidates, `log1p_scaled_lag`, `lag_plus`,
  sparse-panel `intermittent_demand`, and `classical_expert_bank`; its lag
  spine includes rolling mean, optional partial rolling mean, rolling
  standard-deviation, rolling min/max, delta, trend, and calendar features, and
  the auto path adds supported season-length multiples during fit. Candidate
  validation averages deterministic trailing rolling origins when history
  supports them; series-specific auto weights are used only when each series has
  at least four validation points, otherwise prediction falls back to horizon or
  global weights. Selected member refits run in parallel while preserving
  stable roster/member order. The Python wrapper passes `ForecastFrame.static_covariates`
  to the native lag spine by default, supports `covariate_features=[]` as an
  explicit opt-out, and exposes `rich_calendar_features=True`; native EWM target
  features are opt-in via `ewm_alpha_percents` after alpha 90 regressed the
  exact route-mix smoke without validation gating. Rich calendar features include
  day-of-year, elapsed index, an elapsed 14-step phase feature, low-cost
  Fourier weekday/month/day encodings, and month-start/mid/end indicators for
  recurring operational cycles. Route-demand benchmark paths also pass native
  low-cardinality covariate indicators for finite integer-coded static context
  with 3 to 8 distinct training values when a training-only covariate-by-day
  interaction signal clears the fixed threshold, and those indicators can
  participate in the same covariate-calendar interactions.
  Static route covariates and gated indicators may interact with month-start,
  mid-month, and month-end event flags on taxi/synthetic route-demand sources;
  M4/M5/M6 remain on the compact feature path unless separately validated.
  Benchmark claims must cite
  the maintained artifact. The synthetic forecasting suite must not force lag
  solely by source name; lag-spine overrides are limited to documented
  frequency-risk guards and otherwise come from inner-origin validation loss.
  For non-M shared demand sources, auto candidates must also be
  origin-consistent against `cartoboost_lag`; if a candidate loses any usable
  inner validation origin to lag, the benchmark selector keeps lag even when the
  candidate's average inner loss is lower. Non-M inner validation skips the
  raw-auto fit and scores the lag spine plus deterministic shared candidates;
  raw auto remains lazy in outer scoring as well, where the harness starts with
  a placeholder auto column and only materializes the real raw-auto forecast if
  shared selection keeps `cartoboost_auto_forecast`. Rolling-origin suites
  cache identical inner validation cutoffs per problem, keyed by source, cutoff,
  and candidate roster, so overlapping folds reuse the same score without
  changing split semantics.
  Raw native auto must clear the stronger raw-auto override gain before it can
  replace the lag spine on non-M shared demand sources.
- `scripts/forecasting_generalization.py --compact --no-hyperopt` is the
  maintained non-M scalable synthetic external-roster guardrail. It uses the
  fixed synthetic taxi-style suite with 18 series, 150 days, horizon 14, two
  folds, seed 177, the `scalable` roster, and `--no-candidate-selection` so
  benchmark work is not judged only on M4/M5/M6 samples and does not spend
  calibration time when raw native auto and lag are quality-identical.
- `docs/assets/nyc_taxi_benchmarks/forecasting_overhaul_committed_suite_scalable_roster.json`
  is the current scalable external-roster committed synthetic artifact. The
  older full external-roster artifact is still present, but refresh attempts can
  stall in `statsforecast_autotbats`/ARIMA optimization; use the scalable
  artifact for the current external-tree/functime synthetic read.
- Non-M shared candidate selection uses up to two inner rolling origins, with
  an early stop after one origin when `cartoboost_lag` beats every finite
  candidate by at least 15%. M4 keeps three origins, M5 keeps two origins, and
  M6 keeps one; this preserves the current M benchmark artifacts while reducing
  redundant non-M inner fits versus the previous three-origin policy.
- `ClassicalExpertBank` and `AutoStatsBank` rosters include native
  `window_average` candidates with fixed `window_size` values 3 and 7.
  Seasonal rosters also include a native `seasonal_window_average` candidate
  with fixed `window_count=3` when `season_length > 1`; these smooth recent
  levels or matching seasonal positions without adding hyperparameter search.
- Native `CartoBoostLagForecaster` can consume numeric `covariate_features`
  from `ForecastFrame` rows, multiply them by configured calendar features when
  `covariate_calendar_interactions=True`, and carry latest static values
  forward during recursive prediction. The benchmark harness source-gates
  static covariates, covariate-calendar interactions, rich calendar features,
  and rolling-stat enrichments to taxi/synthetic route-demand sources; M4/M5/M6
  stay on the compact competition path unless a targeted benchmark proves
  otherwise.
- M4 suite benchmark orchestration is serial in the committed wrapper so group
  timing stays directly comparable across maintained artifacts.
- Use `objective="rmse_wape"` when the user wants a single validation selector
  that balances RMSE and WAPE; it averages WAPE with RMSE normalized by mean
  absolute actual demand.
- Use `cartoboost.metrics.wrmsse`, `cartoboost.metrics.m6`, and the benchmark
  artifact `official_metrics` section for M5/M6-style checks instead of
  treating all forecasting benchmarks as RMSE-only. M6 benchmark artifacts
  include training-window rank-probability calibration metadata. The maintained
  M5/M6 selector paths avoid nested auto calibration during inner-origin
  scoring to preserve quality while cutting benchmark latency. M5 selection is
  RMSE-first for point quality while still emitting official WRMSSE artifacts;
  M5 shared candidates must beat the lag spine by the configured minimum
  relative-gain guard before replacing it. Two-origin M5 validation keeps only
  the total-reconciled shared route in the
  selectable surface after state/store reconciled variants measured as
  non-selected overhead, and one-origin comparison runs may select the
  autostats/phase-14 point blend. The Rust `ClassicalExpertBank` selects the
  strict best validation-MSE expert rather than a simpler expert inside a 1%
  tolerance; this lets `AutoStatsBank` use validation-winning local statistical
  routes on demand panels. M6 selection is one-origin and RMSE-first for
  the daily-return point proxy, with a validation-selected market-neutral
  return candidate; cite point RMSE and
  calibrated RPS separately because the point-quality route is not the RPS
  leader on the maintained full-roster artifact. M4, M5, and M6 inner-origin
  candidate validation skip expensive raw-auto fits when judging deterministic
  shared candidates; M4 still fits both committed CartoBoost rows in the outer
  reported run, M5 one-origin comparison runs map the skipped outer auto column
  to the already-fit AutoStats candidate, and M6 shared-selection runs use a
  zero-cost market-neutral outer candidate. Direct no-selection runs still fit
  raw auto, and two-origin committed M5 runs still fit raw auto because the
  selected reconciled route depends on it. M6 records skipped raw inner scores
  as null.
- Shared-candidate selection keeps `cartoboost_lag` as the default champion
  unless a replacement candidate clears the configured minimum relative gain
  versus lag. This guard is source-general, runs after native raw-auto keep
  checks, and prevents tiny validation wins from displacing the lag spine.
  Non-M shared demand sources also require origin-consistent displacement:
  any usable inner-origin loss to lag sends auto back to the lag spine. Non-M
  inner validation skips raw-auto fitting up front, while non-M outer scoring
  skips raw-auto fitting unless that exact raw route is the selected final
  candidate.
- After shared-candidate validation selects the final route, benchmark outer
  scoring builds only the required selected candidate columns. Inner validation
  still scores the broad candidate bank, but final artifact generation skips
  unused calendar, trend, reconciliation, and local-statistical columns.
- Forecast benchmark artifacts record separate `cartoboost_lag` and
  `cartoboost_auto_forecast` settings. `--cartoboost-n-estimators` controls the
  lag estimator budget; auto uses the quality floor
  `max(--cartoboost-n-estimators, 360)` unless
  `--cartoboost-auto-n-estimators` is explicitly provided for latency probes.
- Forecasting benchmark artifacts expose `git_commit`, `dataset_hash`,
  `source_file_hashes`, `benchmark_integrity`, and `resource_usage` for
  evidence checks.
- Use `uv sync --group dev` for local development and
  `uv run --group dev maturin develop` after native binding changes.
- Use `npm run typecheck` and `npm run build` for the Docusaurus docs site.
- Run `just validate` for full validation when feasible.
- Benchmark claims need equal settings, serious baselines, stable splits, and
  enough repeatability evidence to support the claim.
